Wolf abundance

[NO_WOLF_001]

Recommended citation: Halbritter, A. H. 2026. Wolf abundance [Ulvebestand] (ID(s): NO_WOLF_001) v. 1.0.0. ecRxiv: https://github.com/NINAnor/ecRxiv/tree/main/indicators/NO_WOLF_001

TipMetadata
TipLog
  • May 15, 2026 - Initial draft for ecRxiv

1. Summary

  1. Background and rationale: The grey wolf (Canis lupus) is the largest member of the dog family and a social species; most wolves live in pairs or packs that defend territories. In Norway the species is found mainly in southeastern parts of the country along the border with Sweden, although individual wolves can roam over long distances and may in principle occur anywhere on the mainland. The species also occurs in large parts of Europe, Asia, and North America. It is listed as critically endangered (kritisk truet) on the Norwegian Red List (2021). Rovdata is responsible for monitoring wolves in Norway. The indicator is part of the Norwegian Nature Index and is described on Naturindeksen.

  2. Methods: The wolf population size is estimated from …. This value is then normalised based on a reference level, which is an expert-assessed population estimate based on known breeding density and the amount of suitable habitat. The indicator is developed in the work with the Norwegian Nature Index (Norwegian Institute for Nature Research and partners 2025). The methods are are described in full on the Nature index web site.

  3. Interpretation: An indicator value of 1 means that the wolf population in the reporting unit is at least as large as expected under the reference condition used in Naturindeksen. Values decreasing toward zero indicate that the population index is further below that reference.

  4. Results and development needs: The indicator is used in Naturindeksen.

1.2 Native language summary - Norwegian

  1. Bakgrunn og begrunnelse: Ulv (Canis lupus) er det største medlemmet av hundefamilien og en sosial art; de fleste lever i par eller flokker som hevder territorier. I Norge finnes arten hovedsakelig i sørøstlige deler av landet ved grensen mot Sverige, men enkeltdyr kan streife langt og kan i prinsippet dukke opp hvor som helst på fastlandet. Arten finnes også i store deler av Europa, Asia og Nord-Amerika, og er vurdert som kritisk truet på Norsk rødliste (2021). Rovdata har ansvar for overvåking av ulv i Norge. Indikatoren inngår i Naturindeksen og er beskrevet på Naturindeksen.

  2. Metoder: … Denne verdien normaliseres deretter basert på en referanseverdi, som er et ekspertvurdert bestandsestimat basert på kjent hekketetthet og mengden egnet habitat. Indikatoren er utviklet i arbeidet med Naturindeksen (Norwegian Institute for Nature Research and partners 2025). Metodene er beskrevet i detalj på Naturindeksens nettsider.

  3. Tolkning: En indikatorverdi på 1 betyr at ulvebestanden i rapporteringsenheten er minst like stor som forventet under referansetilstanden i Naturindeksen. Verdier som nærmer seg null, viser at bestandsindekset ligger lenger under referansen.

  4. Resultater og utviklingsbehov: Indikatoren brukes i Naturindeksen.

2. About the underlying data

The data comes from … The procedure to download the data is described here.

2.1 Spatial and temporal resolution and extent

Indicator values are available for the assessment years in Naturindeksen: 1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, and 2024.

To produce indicator estimates for these years, input is combined over a short window (typically two to three years), as specified in the Naturindeks indicator protocol.

Spatially, data are organised by the eight large-carnivore management regions (forvaltningsregioner for rovvilt, regions 1–8). In Naturindeksen, indicator and reference values are stored at management-region scale (IC units), then aggregated to five geographic regions and national level for reporting, using the same spatial framework as for brown bear and lynx in this repository.

2.2 Sampling design

Rovdata is responsible for monitoring wolves in Norway and for maintaining an overview of how many wolves there are in the country. Populations are estimated from annual winter monitoring, including counts of wolves with wholly Norwegian residency and wolves with residency on both sides of the border with Sweden, and reporting of wolf litters in Norwegian and border territories relative to national targets (Rovdata 2025). Further detail on field methods (for example registration of tracks and signs) is linked from Rovdata.

2.3 Original units

At the monitoring stage, Rovdata reports population status using numbers of wolves (including ranges for a given winter) and documentation of litters in territories (Rovdata 2025).

2.4 Instructions for citing, using and accessing data

Population monitoring and status information: cite Rovdata and the relevant status pages (Rovdata 2025). Methodological documentation for the indicator: Naturindeksen – ulv.

3. Indicator properties

3.1 Ecosystem Condition Typology Class (ECT)

B3 – Functional state characteristics.

3.2 Ecosystem condition characteristic

3.3 Collinearities with other indicators

The indicator may covary with other large-carnivore indicators that use the same regional structure (brown bear, lynx, wolverine) where distributions overlap.

3.4 Impact factors

3.5 Spatial aggregation

Following the metadata pathway Aggregate (population model, large-carnivore management region) – [Scale – Truncate] – Downscale (municipalities) – Aggregate (geographic region) – Aggregate (national level): population estimates or indices are available per forvaltningsregion for rovvilt (IC unit in Naturindeksen). Each unit is normalised against X0 and X100 for that region (linear scaling with truncation to [0, 1]).

Because rovvilt regions and the five Naturindeks geographic regions are two non-aligned partitions of Norway, we use municipalities (BSunits) as a common basic spatial unit. We assign the (scaled) rovvilt-region value to each municipality belonging to that region (uniform-value assumption), hereafter referred to as downscaling. Forest area (Skog) is used as the aggregation weight because wolf is a forest-associated species.

4. Reference condition and levels

4.1 Reference condition

The reference condition for wolf in Naturindeksen is a boreal/montane forest ecosystem with minimal human impact and a climate similar to the reference period used in the Norwegian Nature Index framework (Nybø et al. 2017).

4.2 Reference levels

X100 is set per large-carnivore management region (IC unit in Naturindeksen), using suitable area and density within each rovviltregion and expert assessment: it represents the expected wolf population (or equivalent index) under reference conditions in that unit.

X0 is defined as no wolves in the unit (functional absence).

4.2.1 Spatial resolution and validity

Reference levels and indicator values apply at the scale of forvaltningsregioner for rovvilt (IC units). Aggregated results for the five Naturindeks geographic regions and for Norway are summaries of those units with area weights.

5. Uncertainties

5.1 General uncertainties

Reference levels are held fixed in this workflow (their own uncertainties are not propagated). Regional and national values are area-weighted summaries of forvaltningsregioner for rovvilt, so they inherit model uncertainty at management-region scale and any mismatch between administrative regions and ecological variation within the five Naturindeks geographic regions. Monitoring design and sample effort differ between species and regions; uneven coverage can affect the population models that enter Naturindeksen.

5.2 Reported uncertainties

The intervals in Table 2, Figure 2, and Figure 3 are inferential: they describe uncertainty around the estimated area-weighted mean indicator for each reporting unit (a geographic region or Norway as a whole), given the statistical models used for management-region abundance in Naturindeksen.

For each management region and assessment year, Naturindeks provides a predictive distribution for the population index (on the variable scale before scaling). We draw nsim = 1000 values per region, scale each draw to the 0–1 indicator scale using the regional reference level and linear scaling, then downscale by assigning each scaled draw to every municipality in that region and aggregate with ecTools::ec_upscale(): first from municipalities (BSunits) to each NI geographic region (part), then from regions to Norway, using forest area (Skog) as the weight. The 1000 sampled_mean values per region and year describe inferential uncertainty for the forest-area-weighted mean indicator; we summarise them with the median and 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles as an approximate 95% interval, and the 25% and 75% quantiles as an approximate interquartile range.

6. References

Norwegian Institute for Nature Research, and partners. 2025. “Norwegian Nature Index (Naturindeks).” https://www.naturindeks.no.
Nybø, Signe et al. 2017. “Fagsystem for Naturindeks: Referansetilstand Og Skalering.” Norwegian Institute for Nature Research (NINA).
Rovdata. 2025. “Bestandsstatus: Ulv.” https://rovdata.no/Ulv/Bestandsstatus.aspx.

7. Import and prepare datasets

Run the download chunk once to refresh the local export. This project’s renv environment installs NIcalc from GitHub ChloeRN/NIcalc, branch include_ReadOnly_access (see renv.lock and install_deps.R); that build defaults to the Naturindeks read-only API user, so you do not need username / password arguments here. When NINAnor/NIcalc PR #59 is merged into NINAnor/NIcalc, you can switch the renv install back to the main repo and update the lockfile. We save the RDS export under data/ so that we do not need to download the data every time we run the analysis.

Download wolf data from Naturindeks API 
wolfImport <- NIcalc::importDatasetApi(
  eco = NULL,
  indic = c("Ulv"),
  year = c("1990", "2000", "2010", "2014", "2019", "2024")
)

saveRDS(wolfImport, here::here("data/wolfImport.rds"))

8. Spatial units

According to Naturindeks terminology, municipalities are BSunits. The eight forvaltningsregioner for rovvilt (rovvilt regions 1–8) are subsamples of BSunits and are referred to as ICunits in the import; they are the IC units for this indicator. The five Naturindeks geographic regions (east, south, west, central, north; codes E, S, W, C, N) are parts and NIunits, taken from the part field on municipality-level BSunits in the same import (see Section 9 for downscaling and aggregation). Mainland Norway is the ecosystem accounting area.

Code 
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("img/rovviltregioner.png"))

9. Analyses

This section uses the following workflow: we take variable values and reference levels from the Naturindeks import, normalise at large-carnivore management-region scale (IC unit) with linear scaling, then downscale by assigning the scaled values to the municipalities in each region and propagate uncertainty and aggregate spatially with forest-area weights using ecTools::ec_upscale(). At geographic (NI part) and national scale, uncertainties are inferential: each replicate is a forest-area-weighted mean across municipalities (then, for Norway, one draw per NI region). We use linear scaling and forest area (Skog) from the Naturindeks spatial framework as the weight.

Management-region population indices in the database are stored as predictive distributions, not only as point estimates. Reference levels per region (X0 = 0, X100 from the download) are applied before any geographic or national aggregation. We ignore uncertainty in the reference levels themselves.

Assemble import and indicator object

assembleNiObject() reshapes wolfImport so that indicator observations are in one list element per assessment year, and so that underlying spatial units (BSunits) are aligned with the thematic index structure used in Naturindeks.

Assemble wolf NI object (thematic, predefined regions) 
wolfAssemble <- NIcalc::assembleNiObject(
  inputData = wolfImport,
  predefNIunits = c(allArea = T, parts = T, counties = F),
  indexType = "thematic"
)

Check input data ..... 

Elicitate incomplete reference values and indicator observations ..... 

Assemble dataset .....

Reference levels (X0 and X100)

Before scaling, we read regional reference levels from referenceValues in the RDS export (rows with yearId == 0 or reference year name). For this indicator, X0 is zero individuals in the unit and X100 is the expert reference population index per rovvilt region (expectedValue in the reference slice).

Management-region reference levels from downloaded Naturindeks data 
refs <- wolfImport$referenceValues$referenceValues |> 
  dplyr::filter(.data$yearId == 0L | grepl("Referanse", .data$yearName, fixed = FALSE)) |>
  dplyr::distinct(ICunitId, ICunitName, x100 = expectedValue) |>
  dplyr::mutate(x0 = 0)
refs |>
  gt::gt()
Management-region reference levels (X0 = 0, X100) by rovvilt region (IC unit).
ICunitId ICunitName x100 x0
3793 Rovviltregion 8 528 0
3795 Rovviltregion 7 248 0
3796 Rovviltregion 6 412 0
3799 Rovviltregion 1 287 0
3803 Rovviltregion 2 287 0
3807 Rovviltregion 3 163 0
3809 Rovviltregion 4 44 0
3811 Rovviltregion 5 202 0

Resample non-scaled management-region variables

For each assessment year, Naturindeks stores one value per rovvilt region as a parameterised distribution (or a custom distribution). We draw nsim = 1000 random values per region with NIcalc::sampleObsMat(). Rows with a standard distribution family use observation type tradObs; rows with only a custom distribution use customObs.

Assessment years are 1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, and 2024. The result mgmt_draws is a long table of simulated population indices (value) by ICunitId and year, not yet on the 0–1 indicator scale.

Observation types for sampleObsMat 
myYears <- as.character(c(1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, 2024))
obstype <- vector("list", length(myYears))
for (i in seq_along(myYears)) {
  obs <- wolfAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distributionFamilyName
  obs[!is.na(obs)] <- "tradObs"
  obs[is.na(obs)] <- "customObs"
  obstype[[i]] <- obs
}
Resample management-region variables (non-scaled) 
mgmt_draws <- tibble(ICunitId = NULL, value = NULL, year = NULL)

for (i in seq_along(myYears)) {
  myMat <- NIcalc::sampleObsMat(
    ICunitId = wolfAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$ICunitId,
    value = wolfAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$expectedValue,
    distrib = wolfAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distributionFamilyName,
    mu = wolfAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distParameter1,
    sig = wolfAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$distParameter2,
    customDistribution = wolfAssemble$indicatorValues[[i]]$customDistribution,
    obsType = obstype[[i]],
    nsim = 1000
  )
  ICunitIds <- row.names(myMat)
  colnames(myMat) <- paste0("s", seq_len(ncol(myMat)))
  myMat <- myMat |>
    as_tibble() |>
    add_column(ICunitId = ICunitIds, .before = 1) |>
    pivot_longer(cols = -any_of("ICunitId"), names_to = "sim_col", values_to = "value") |>
    mutate(
      sim_id = readr::parse_number(.data$sim_col),
      year = myYears[i]
    ) |>
    select(ICunitId, sim_id, value, year)

  mgmt_draws <- mgmt_draws |>
    bind_rows(myMat)
}
head(mgmt_draws) |>
  gt::gt()
Preview of resampled management-region variable values (first rows).
ICunitId sim_id value year
3793 1 0 1990
3793 2 0 1990
3793 3 0 1990
3793 4 0 1990
3793 5 0 1990
3793 6 0 1990

Scaling to indicator values (linear)

Normalisation is done at management-region scale before any geographic or national aggregation. Each draw is joined to its regional x0 and x100, then mapped to [0, 1] with ecTools::ec_normalise() using linear increasing scaling from X0 to X100 (no upper-tail unimodal anchor).

Join reference levels and linear scaling to indicator scale 
mgmt_draws <- mgmt_draws |>
  left_join(
    refs |> mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId)),
    by = join_by(ICunitId)
  )

ind <- ec_normalise(
  variable = mgmt_draws$value,
  x0 = mgmt_draws$x0,
  x100 = mgmt_draws$x100,
  fun = "linear"
)

mgmt_draws <- mgmt_draws |> add_column(indicator = ind)

The scatter plot below (Figure 2) shows the mapping from variable to indicator for each rovvilt region (one facet per ICunitName). The figure is saved when the document is rendered.

Save normalisation diagnostic figure (English) 
wolf_plot <- ec_norm_plot(mgmt_draws$value, mgmt_draws$indicator) +
  facet_wrap(. ~ mgmt_draws$ICunitName)
ggsave(filename = here::here("img/wolf_norm_plot_eng.png"), plot = wolf_plot, scale = 1.5)
Saving 10.5 x 7.5 in image
Code 
knitr::include_graphics(here::here("img/wolf_norm_plot_eng.png"))

Aggregation weights (forest area by municipality)

Spatial aggregation uses forest area (Skog) on each underlying municipality in BSunits. Municipalities are linked to rovvilt regions via ICunits, and each BSunit carries a Naturindeks geographic region code (part: E, S, W, C, N). Rovvilt regions and NI parts are two non-aligned partitions of Norway, so we keep the data at municipality level once it is scaled: each municipality inherits the scaled indicator value of its rovvilt region (uniform-value assumption), and is then aggregated upward with its own forest area.

Forest area, NI part, and IC link per municipality 
mgmt_weights <- as_tibble(wolfAssemble$BSunits) |>
  rename(BSunitId = id)

IC_BC_link <- wolfImport$ICunits |>
  distinct(ICunitId = as.character(id), BSunitId)

mgmt_weights <- mgmt_weights |>
  left_join(IC_BC_link, by = join_by(BSunitId)) |>
  mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId))

Downscale the scaled indicator values from each rovvilt region by assigning them to every municipality in that region. The result is a long table with one row per (year, sim_id, BSunitId) carrying its rovvilt region’s indicator draw, its part, and the municipality’s Skog.

Downscale rovvilt-region draws to municipalities 
mgmt_draws <- mgmt_draws |>
  mutate(ICunitId = as.character(ICunitId))

bsunit_draws <- mgmt_draws |>
  right_join(
    mgmt_weights |> select(BSunitId, ICunitId, part, Skog),
    by = join_by(ICunitId),
    unmatched = "error",
    relationship = "many-to-many"
  )

Spatial aggregation

Aggregate from municipalities to NI geographic regions with ecTools::ec_upscale(), using Monte Carlo propagation of inferential uncertainty (one draw per municipality per replicate, forest-area-weighted mean).

Municipality to NI part MC propagation (ec_upscale) 
mc_propagated <- ecTools::ec_upscale(
  data = bsunit_draws,
  variable = indicator,
  weight = Skog,
  start_units = BSunitId,
  end_units = part,
  year = year,
  end_units_name = "part",
  n = 1000
)
saveRDS(mc_propagated, here::here("data/wolf_mc_propagated_regional.rds"))

For the national indicator, propagate again from regions to Norway. Summed forest area per geographic region is used as the weight at this step.

Total forest area per NI region 
region_weights <- mgmt_weights |>
  group_by(part) |>
  summarise(forest_area = sum(.data$Skog, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
region_weights
# A tibble: 5 × 2
  part  forest_area
  <chr>       <dbl>
1 C          20568.
2 E          39436.
3 N          30469.
4 S          18229.
5 W          11479.
NI region to Norway MC propagation (ec_upscale) 
mc_propagated_for_national <- mc_propagated |>
  left_join(region_weights, by = join_by(part)) |>
  add_column(end_units = "Norway")

mc_propagated_national <- ecTools::ec_upscale(
  data = mc_propagated_for_national,
  variable = sampled_mean,
  weight = forest_area,
  start_units = part,
  end_units = end_units,
  year = year,
  end_units_name = "part",
  n = 1000
)
saveRDS(mc_propagated_national, here::here("data/wolf_mc_propagated_national.rds"))

Regional and national MC draws are combined for summarisation and export.

Bind regional and national MC outputs 
mc_propagated_combined <- mc_propagated |>
  select(year, part, sampled_mean) |>
  bind_rows(mc_propagated_national)

10. Results

From the 1000 Monte Carlo replicates per region and year in mc_propagated_combined, we report the median as the point estimate and use the 2.5% and 97.5% quantiles as an approximate 95% interval for the forest-area-weighted mean indicator (inferential uncertainty, not descriptive spread between management regions). The table (Table 2) and time series (Figure 3) summarise regional and national (Norway) values.

Code 
summary_out <- mc_propagated_combined |>
  mutate(part = dplyr::recode(part, !!!wolf_part_labels)) |>
  group_by(part, year) |>
  summarise(
    median = round(median(sampled_mean, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    p_025 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.025, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    p_25 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.25, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    p_75 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.75, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    p_975 = round(quantile(sampled_mean, probs = 0.975, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(part = factor(part, levels = wolf_region_levels)) |>
  add_column(
    ECT = "B3",
    Indicator = "Wolf abundance",
    Ecosystem = "Boreal/montane forests",
    ID = "NO_WOLF_001"
  )
Code 
wolf_plot_years <- as.character(c(1990, 2000, 2010, 2014, 2019, 2024))
wolf_region_cols <- setNames(
  MetBrewer::met.brewer("Archambault", n = length(wolf_region_levels)),
  wolf_region_levels
)

plot_data <- summary_out |>
  mutate(
    year = factor(year, levels = wolf_plot_years),
    series_type = if_else(part == "Norway", "National", "Region")
  )

y_hi <- max(plot_data$p_975, na.rm = TRUE)
y_max <- min(1, max(0.08, y_hi * 1.12, na.rm = TRUE))
y_breaks <- pretty(c(0, y_max), n = 6)

ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = year, y = median, colour = part, group = part)) +
  geom_line(
    aes(linewidth = series_type),
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.18)
  ) +
  geom_pointrange(
    aes(ymin = p_025, ymax = p_975, shape = series_type),
    size = 0.8,
    linewidth = 0.45,
    position = position_dodge(width = 0.18)
  ) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = wolf_region_cols, name = NULL) +
  scale_shape_manual(
    values = c(National = 19, Region = 17),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  scale_linewidth_manual(
    values = c(National = 1, Region = 0.55),
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(0, y_max),
    breaks = y_breaks,
    expand = ggplot2::expansion(mult = c(0.02, 0.04))
  ) +
  theme_bw(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.box = "vertical",
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  ) +
  guides(
    colour = guide_legend(nrow = 2, override.aes = list(size = 0.7, linewidth = 0.45)),
    shape = guide_legend(override.aes = list(size = 0.8, linewidth = 0.45))
  ) +
  labs(y = "Indicator value", x = "")
Code 
summary_out |>
  arrange(part, year) |>
  unite(col = "CI_95", c(p_025, p_975), sep = " - ") |>
  unite(col = "IQR", c(p_25, p_75), sep = " - ") |>
  select(
    ECT,
    Indicator,
    ID,
    "Accounting area" = part,
    Ecosystem,
    "Closing year" = year,
    "Indicator value" = median,
    "95% CI" = CI_95,
    "50% CI" = IQR
  ) |>
  kable()

11. Export file

Regional and national Monte Carlo draws are exported in one table (mc_propagated_combined: year, part, sampled_mean; part includes NI regions and Norway).

Export regional and national MC-propagated indicator draws 
write.csv(
  mc_propagated_combined,
  here::here("data/results_NO_WOLF_001_v1.0.0.csv"),
  row.names = FALSE
)

12. Author contributions

Not relevant, as this is the work of a single author. I would, however, like to acknowledge the work of those that created the indicator on Naturindeks (Norwegian Institute for Nature Research and partners 2025).

TipSession Info

This information is mainly aimed at developers that need to recreate the R environment that produced the html. Important note: this information is about the R session that was running when the html was rendered, and if some of the code chunks were not evaluated when the html was rendered, they may have been executed in a different environment.
